behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels
behaviors.justification: This tests pure logic: does the module construct a request payload that includes all taxonomy labels? A mock of the external API call can verify the request arguments. No I/O, subprocess, or real API needed—just verify the code builds the correct payload with all labels present.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API call and capture the request arguments. Call the classification module with a document. Assert that the request payload includes every label from the configured taxonomy in the expected format.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label not in the taxonomy, the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value
behaviors.justification: This is straightforward validation logic. Mock the API to return an out-of-taxonomy label (e.g., 'spam'). The module must catch it and return 'unknown'. The code logic is local and deterministic; a mock can inject the invalid label and verify the response without needing real infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a label not in the taxonomy. Call the classification module with a valid document. Assert that the module returns 'unknown' rather than forwarding the unexpected label to the caller.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls
behaviors.justification: This likely tests caching logic. Mock the API and call the module twice with the same document, then verify the mock was called exactly once (second call used cached result). If the module makes 2 API calls instead of 1, the test catches it. No real infrastructure needed to verify the caching mechanism works.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a specific label. Call the classification module twice with identical document input. Assert that the external API was called exactly once (not twice), proving the second call used a cached result. Verify both calls returned identical results.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A mock token counter could return unrealistic values (e.g., 500 tokens) while real token counting (via tiktoken or similar) produces 5,000+. The mock test would pass, but production would fail on budget overages. Real token counting must be used—either a local tokenizer library (Integration) or an external API (System). Local library is more likely.
behaviors.test_description: Using the actual local token counting library or SDK, create test documents of increasing size including a typical document. Call the classification module with each. Assert that no document exceeding the per-call token budget is sent to the external API. Verify the module either rejects, truncates, or handles oversized requests according to policy.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller
behaviors.justification: This tests the retry/error-handling logic. Mock the API to return a rate-limit error on the first call, then success on retry. Verify the module returns the eventual success result rather than propagating the rate-limit error. The logic is local control flow; a mock can inject the error state and verify the module's decision to retry.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return a rate-limit error (e.g., 429) on the first call and a successful response on retry. Call the classification module with a document. Assert that the module retries after the rate-limit error and returns the successful classification result to the caller, not the error.
